narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 28 (well under the average 36.7), and low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 grades at school—and predicted an 86.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were, first, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 (far below the average of about 5.5), which strongly signaled to the model a drop-off in performance compared to peers. Second, your Year 1 school decile of 1 (versus the average around 5.7) reinforced concerns about consistently underperforming relative to other students at your level. Third, your LSAT score contributed by being on the lower end, nudging the odds further toward failure rather than success. While your plan to attend full-time (like most applicants at 92.7%) was neutral or slightly favorable, it couldn't offset these top concerns, which stood out as bigger drags than what's typical for approved candidates. In contrast, students who get accepted usually have stronger rankings and test scores that pull their failure risk down below 50%. This doesn't define your potential, but it highlights where the model saw the biggest gaps in your profile compared to successful bar passers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through post-bac courses or a master's program, and seeking targeted academic advising to improve your school performance metrics. Building a clearer path to higher achievement could help you reapply stronger next cycle, as many students in similar situations have done successfully.
